title: One Day of Life
description: A novel about repression and daily survival during El Salvador's political violence.
descriptionParagraphs: A novel about repression and daily survival during El Salvador's political violence.

One Day of Life is usually read through its treatment of civil conflict, state violence, and political dissent. As a novel, it turns those concerns into conflicts of character, voice, setting, and social pressure rather than leaving them as abstract ideas.

Part of the work's durability lies in the way its form intensifies its themes. Readers return to it not only for subject matter but for the distinctive voice, structure, and atmosphere through which it makes civil conflict, state violence, and political dissent feel immediate.

cover.alt: One Day of Life cover thumbnail from Wikipedia

overview.englishDescription: A novel about repression and daily survival during El Salvador's political violence.
banningParagraphs: One Day of Life entered censorship debates as a novel associated with civil conflict, state violence, and political dissent. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around political dissent and anti state.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1980s in El Salvador, where Salvadoran authorities banned circulation. The novel's attention to repression made it unacceptable to the state it depicted. Its suppression belongs to the wider censorship of civil-war testimony in Central America.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism
counterReadings.author: Hannah Arendt
counterReadings.note: A foundational analysis of state terror, propaganda, and ideological conformity.

counterReadings.title: On Tyranny
counterReadings.author: Timothy Snyder
counterReadings.note: A short modern guide to resisting authoritarian politics and controlled public discourse.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
